title: Prumnopitys utilior
intro: Prumnopitys taxifolia, commonly known as the Miro or Prumnopitys utilior, is a coniferous tree native to New Zealand, characterized by its tall, straight trunk and dark green, needle-like leaves. The Miro tree has been used in traditional Maori medicine for centuries, with reported health benefits including anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, which may help to alleviate respiratory issues and skin infections. The bioactive constituents of Prumnopitys taxifolia include terpenoids, flavonoids, and phenolic acids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. In herbal medicine, the Miro tree is commonly used to make teas, infusions, and tinctures, which are used to treat a range of conditions, including bronchitis, asthma, and eczema.
taxonomy: Prumnopitys utilior is classified as a species of coniferous tree in the family Podocarpaceae. It is native to New Zealand. This species is a member of the monotypic genus Prumnopitys. Prumnopitys utilior is commonly known as the Māori cedar or the New Zealand cedar. It is a highly valued timber tree due to its durability and attractive appearance.

The plant's leaves and bark are used in traditional medicine to treat fever and rheumatism.
